data.area: Quantitative model — EAL/ROSI expectation math (outputs/08, README Task 4, exec summary, dashboard, SOURCES.md, advanced_analytics.py)

defects.what: Residual EAL is computed with a cross-paired vertex average instead of the correct expectation of a product of two independent triangulars. The shipped code uses resid_mean = (bl*(1-eh) + bm*(1-em) + bh*(1-el))/3, which is NOT E[baseline*(1-eff)]. Because baseline and effectiveness are independent triangular draws, the correct value is E[baseline]*E[1-eff] = mean_b*(1-mean_e). A 1.5M-draw brute-force Monte Carlo confirms the true value matches mean_b*(1-mean_e), not the cross-paired formula. The cross-pairing overstates residual EAL by 20-29% per risk (R3 +28.8%, R1 +28.8%, R2 +22.7%, R4 +20.2%, R5 +20.0%), which understates avoided EAL and therefore understates ROSI.
defects.where: workflow/advanced_analytics.py:198 (committed HEAD): `resid_mean = (bl * (1 - eh / 100) + bm * (1 - em / 100) + bh * (1 - el / 100)) / 3.0`
defects.proof: For R3 (b=8/20/40, e=45/70/85): shipped resid_mean=9.733; correct mean_b*(1-mean_e)=(22.667)*(1-66.667/100)=7.556; independent Monte Carlo (1.5M draws) E[B(1-E)]=7.557. The shipped basis string (advanced_analytics.py:213 and outputs/08 eal_basis) explicitly claims 'probability = triangular mean (not mode)', but the cross-paired average is not the residual's mean, so the code contradicts its own stated method.
defects.fix: Replace line 198 with `resid_mean = prob_base_mean * (1 - (el+em+eh)/3.0/100)` and regenerate outputs/08. (Note: an uncommitted working-tree edit already applies exactly this fix, but it is NOT committed and none of the shipped numbers reflect it.)

defects.what: Portfolio ROSI and residual EAL headlines are wrong (understated) as a downstream consequence of the cross-pairing bug. The v1.1 headline 'ROSI ≈ 258%' and 'Residual EAL $1.01M' are incorrect; with the correct product-of-means expectation the same code produces ROSI 317% and residual EAL $795,175 ($0.80M). v1.0 is likewise 333%→395% and residual EAL $1,116,533→$891,859.
defects.where: README.md:13 and :202 ('ROSI ≈ 258%', '$1.01M'); outputs/07_executive_summary.md:1 ('ROSI ≈ 258%', '$1.01M'); dashboard.html (258%, $1.01M); outputs/08_advanced_analytics.json versions.v1.1_research_calibrated.eal_rosi.portfolio (rosi_pct 258, eal_residual_usd 1007438)
defects.proof: Re-running the committed advanced_analytics.py with only the eal_rosi residual line corrected to mean_b*(1-mean_e) prints: v1.1 'EAL $2,301,500 -> $795,175 | ROSI 317%'; v1.0 'EAL $2,682,666 -> $891,859 | ROSI 395%'. The baseline EAL ($2.30M v1.1 / $2.68M v1.0) and the −13.6% mode-vs-mean baseline correction are correct; only the residual leg is wrong.
defects.fix: After fixing the formula, update the headline residual EAL to ~$0.80M and ROSI to ~317% (v1.1) in README, 07, dashboard, SOURCES.md; regenerate 08. Direction note: the error is conservative (it understates the benefit), but it is still a provable math error the deliverable presents as a rigorous expectation.

data.summary: Independently re-derived every computed number from raw parameters in fresh Python (no import of the repo's analytics). Scoring, the deterministic quant model (v1.0 and v1.1), aggregate/marginal/bounds, tornado, framework sensitivity, the ~0.40 correlation claim, loss-exceedance, and risk-appetite verdicts ALL reproduce exactly — the deliverable is arithmetically sound almost everywhere. The one real defect is exactly the one the prompt flagged: residual EAL is computed as a cross-paired vertex average (bl*(1-eh)+bm*(1-em)+bh*(1-el))/3 rather than the correct expectation of a product of independent triangulars, mean_b*(1-mean_e). A 1.5M-draw Monte Carlo confirms the true residual matches mean_b*(1-mean_e), not the shipped formula; the cross-pairing overstates residual EAL by 20-29% per risk. This makes the shipped v1.1 headline ROSI 258% / residual EAL $1.01M wrong — the corrected code (same script, one line) yields ROSI 317% / $0.80M (v1.0: 333%->395%), and every per-risk ROSI (incl. the thrice-quoted 'R5 40%', correctly 65%) is understated. The error is conservative (understates the program's benefit) but is a provable expectation-math error the deliverable presents as rigorous, and the committed code's own basis string ('probability = triangular mean') contradicts what it computes. Note: an UNCOMMITTED working-tree edit already fixes exactly this line, but it is not committed and none of the shipped JSON/README/dashboard/exec-summary numbers reflect it — the shipped (HEAD) state is the buggy one.

data.summary: The demo is highly internally consistent on its headline v1.1 numbers, the deterministic pipeline (replay PASS, analytics reproduces outputs/08), flag/agent counts, ranking, and EAL/ROSI/loss-exceedance figures — I verified these and they hold. The real defects cluster in two places. (1) The SOC 2 evidence matrix is transcribed inconsistently across the three documents: README's R2 cell drops CC6.3 and its R3 cell drops A1.3 — the very two criteria the audit claims to have ADDED — while the dashboard drops CC7.4 from R2, and both README/dashboard render R1 as 'CC7.2–7.4' implying a CC7.3 that has no row in the authoritative matrix. No two documents agree on R2's audited set. (2) The reliability-scorecard price count ('10 of 13, 3 corrected') doesn't match SOURCES.md, which has 11 items with 5 corrections marked; and the v1.0 'biggest lever' is stated as 11.7 pp in the exec summary and SOURCES.md but the as-run artifact (outputs/08) says 11.8, with two more exec-summary v1.0 marginals (R2 5.5 vs 5.6, R5 2.1 vs 2.2) off by 0.1. The SOC 2 discrepancies are the most damaging because they contradict the deliverable's own 'dropped 2 / added 2' boast; the price-count and 11.7/11.8 issues are smaller but are exactly the kind of self-inconsistency a sharp evaluator will catch since the source artifacts are shipped alongside.

data.area: Code correctness — advanced_analytics.py, replay_harness.mjs, risk_pipeline.workflow.js, schemas.json (agentic-risk-prioritization)

defects.what: Residual EAL is computed with a vertex-pairing average that is NOT the mean of the residual distribution, contradicting the function's own stated basis ('probability = triangular mean') and the model's independence assumption. eal_rosi() computes resid_mean = (bl*(1-eh/100) + bm*(1-em/100) + bh*(1-el/100))/3, pairing the low baseline vertex with the high effectiveness vertex (perfect negative correlation). Baseline and effectiveness are independent everywhere else in the model, so E[residual] = E[b]*E[1-e] = mean_b*(1-mean_e). The vertex-average overstates residual EAL by ~20-37% per risk, which understates EAL-avoided and therefore understates ROSI.
defects.where: workflow/advanced_analytics.py:198 (resid_mean = (bl * (1 - eh / 100) + bm * (1 - em / 100) + bh * (1 - el / 100)) / 3.0); stated basis contradicted at line 213 ("probability = triangular mean") and line 350
defects.proof: 400k-iteration Monte Carlo of b*(1-e/100) with independent triangular b and e (the model's own assumption) gives E[residual]: R1 7.552, R3 5.664, R5 3.993. product_of_means (mean_b*(1-mean_e)) reproduces these exactly (R1 7.556, R3 5.667, R5 3.990). The shipped vertex-average gives R1 9.733, R3 7.767, R5 4.760 — wrong by +29%, +37%, +19%. Correcting it raises v1.1 ROSI from R3 528%->671%, R1 330%->370%, R2 180%->232%, R4 185%->219%, R5 40%->65% and portfolio 258%->317% (EAL residual $1.01M->$0.80M). The baseline leg (line 196, prob_base_mean=(bl+bm+bh)/3) IS the correct triangular mean, so only the residual leg is inconsistent with the stated basis.
defects.fix: Compute residual mean as the product of independent means: eff_mean=(el+em+eh)/3; resid_mean = prob_base_mean * (1 - eff_mean/100). Then regenerate outputs/08_advanced_analytics.json and update the inherited ROSI/EAL figures in README.md (lines 13, 202, 204) and dashboard.html (lines 130, 162, and the hardcoded rosi values at 244/248/252/256/260) to match.

verified_correct: Determinism: two consecutive clean runs of python3 workflow/advanced_analytics.py produce byte-identical outputs/08_advanced_analytics.json (MD5 560560524fd79ca7bf7cacdee62174b7 both times); no diff vs the committed 08. The seed (SEED=20260706) and the per-version random.Random(SEED) reseed make the Monte Carlo fully reproducible.

Per-version RNG isolation is real: running v1.1's monte_carlo with a fresh Random(SEED) yields identical results whether or not v1.0's monte_carlo+loss_exceedance consumed an rng first — each version reseeds, so v1.1 numbers do not depend on run order.

Replay harness genuinely proves reproducibility of the deterministic code outputs. Mutation tests all behaved correctly: mutating ranking[0].composite in 02 -> FAIL exit code 1; mutating quant.model.aggregate in 05 -> FAIL exit 1; mutating a non-revised raw_estimate baseline (flows through recompute) -> FAIL exit 1. The clean run PASSes with 24 agent calls replayed and byte-identical ranking/table/model/final_estimates/top_risk. (Blind spots — mutating an R5 raw_estimate that is overridden by revised, or editing the LLM-authored 07 exec summary — are correctly outside the harness's stated scope of deterministic-code outputs, not defects.)

tri_inv(u,a,c,b) edge cases correct: b<=a degenerate (a==b==c) returns a; u=0 and u=1 return the low/high bounds; the fc boundary is continuous; a==c (fc=0) and c==b (fc=1) spikes handled without division-by-zero or NaN.
pct() interpolation correct: single-element returns that element; q=0 and q=1 return min/max; q=0.5 on [1,2,3,4] returns 2.5 (linear interpolation on index q*(n-1)).
tornado residual_at_all_modes_pct (20.5) equals the deterministic aggregate residual_mode_pct (20.5); marginal_contribution deltas sum consistently against base 56.7.

LEC severity multiplier triangular(0.5, 1.0, 2.5) is honestly labeled as an assumption, not calibrated: README line 206 'a stated fat-tail multiplier', code docstring lines 226/351 'a stated triangular multiplier ... drives the risk-appetite check only'; NetDiligence is cited only as directional motivation, not as calibrating the specific values.

Deterministic workflow model math (risk_pipeline.workflow.js) reproduces the shipped ranking, scoring table, quant model and final estimates exactly (verified via replay). Residual bounds pairing (low_baseline x high_effectiveness for best case, high_baseline x low_effectiveness for worst case) is the correct bounding convention for a bounds display and is labeled as such.
README/dashboard headline numbers that do NOT depend on the buggy leg are correct against computed 08: v1.1 baseline 56.7%, correlated residual band 17.6-39.9%, LEC P(loss>$2M) 52.8%->20.8%.

parallel()/pipeline() catch-and-return-null in the harness and the workflow retry wrapper do not silently corrupt results — a dropped item would surface as a mismatch in the replay diff or a missing risk; the clean replay shows 5/5 everywhere.
data.summary: The demo's reproducibility machinery is sound: advanced_analytics.py is fully deterministic and reproduces the shipped outputs/08 byte-for-byte, the per-version reseed genuinely isolates the two models, the replay harness correctly FAILS (exit 1) on mutated deterministic outputs, and tri_inv/pct handle their edge cases. The LEC fat-tail multiplier is honestly labeled as an assumption. One real math defect survives: eal_rosi() computes the residual expected loss as a vertex-pairing average (workflow/advanced_analytics.py:198) instead of the product of independent means the model assumes and the function's own eal_basis claims. Monte Carlo ground truth confirms the shipped residual EAL is overstated ~20-37% per risk, so every ROSI figure in the README and dashboard (v1.1 portfolio 258%, R3 528%, etc.) is understated by that flaw and should be ~317% portfolio / R3 671% under the correct independent-means computation. It is internally consistent (same wrong number everywhere) but wrong against the stated method, and a reviewer who recomputes E[residual] will catch it.

data.summary: I fact-checked every load-bearing external claim in the demo against primary/authoritative 2024-2026 sources (Proofpoint, Google 2019 security-key study, Verizon DBIR 2026, GitGuardian 2026, Sophos 2025, NetDiligence 2025 primary PDF, IBM CoDB 2025, Microsoft Tycoon2FA blog, cyber-insurance underwriting, and all 13 vendor pricing items). Every single one verified — figures, wording, dates, and attributions are accurate. Notably: the two NetDiligence figures that secondary coverage does not surface ($142K Nano-Rev average and $2.1M BI-ransomware total) were confirmed by directly extracting text from the primary NetDiligence 2025 PDF (Nano-Rev N=4,009 avg $142K max $10.4M; 'average BI cost for ransomware incidents was $1.4M with a total incident cost of $2.1M'). The Google study wording matches verbatim, the Proofpoint 62%/65% pair is correctly attributed, DBIR 2026's 48%/31%/96% all hold, GitGuardian's 28.65M/64% hold, Sophos $638,536 is exact, IBM $160/record and the 'CoDB 2026 not yet published' caveat are correct, Tycoon2FA's 500K-orgs/push-bypass claims match Microsoft's own March 2026 blog, and every price (Defender Suite $12 rename, Entra P2 $10, GitHub Secret Protection $19 on Team, Front Door Premium $330, YubiKey $58/$29, Postgres Entra $0) matches live vendor pages. No factual, misattribution, or overstatement defects were found in the external claim base. This is an unusually rigorous and defensible set of citations; the demo's own 'human should click through' hedge is warranted but the substance holds. Zero defects to report.

data.area: Security-framework mappings (MITRE ATT&CK + SOC 2 / AICPA TSC) in ROADMAP.md, cross-checked against outputs/09_research_validation.json

defects.what: The demo's flagship MITRE 'correction' is factually wrong: it claims 'ATT&CK has no precise "dump a SQL database" Collection technique' and replaces the R2/R5 database-collection mapping with prose. MITRE ATT&CK T1213.006 'Data from Information Repositories: Databases' exists (created 22 May 2025, current in v19.1), and explicitly names PostgreSQL, Azure SQL Database, MySQL, MongoDB, RDS, Snowflake as in scope — the exact fit for R2 (web-app DB dump) and R5 (direct-Postgres dump). The 'correct' technique existed the whole time.
defects.where: ROADMAP.md:55 ('ATT&CK has no precise "dump a SQL database" Collection technique') and the prose placeholders at ROADMAP.md:60 ('no precise ATT&CK Collection ID for a SQL dump') and ROADMAP.md:63 ('collection via the dormant account's own DB access')
defects.proof: attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1213/006/ — 'Data from Information Repositories: Databases', ID T1213.006, Version 1.0, Created 22 May 2025, Last Modified 12 May 2026, not deprecated; page text names 'MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Amazon Relational Database Service, Azure SQL Database, ... and Snowflake' and lists FIN6/APT41 procedure examples against SQL Server/Oracle. It is a sub-technique of T1213 under the Collection tactic. The demo also mis-scopes the parent: ROADMAP.md:55 says MITRE 'scopes T1213 to SharePoint/Confluence/wikis/code repos — not a backend relational database,' but the T1213 parent page itself lists 'IaaS databases, enterprise databases' and AWS RDS in scope. Both the sub-technique fit and the parent-scope claim are wrong.
defects.fix: Map R2 and R5 database-collection steps to [T1213.006](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1213/006/) Data from Information Repositories: Databases; delete the 'no precise ATT&CK Collection ID' sentence in ROADMAP.md:55 and the two prose placeholders at :60 and :63; correct the false claim that T1213 excludes relational/backend databases.

defects.what: Internal contradiction between the as-run research output and the ROADMAP narrative. Neither artifact acknowledges the actually-correct sub-technique T1213.006, so the demo simultaneously calls T1213 'verified' and 'a misfit,' and the 'second expert pass' still landed on a wrong answer.

defects.proof: JSON lines 389/474 record id 'T1213' as the mapped/verified technique for R2 and R5; line 513 summary says all mappings 'verified ... none deprecated or revoked.' ROADMAP.md:55 contradicts this by declaring the same T1213 mapping a misfit. The reconciling correct value (T1213.006) appears in neither file.

The official name is 'Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol'; 'Asymmetric' is the defining word that distinguishes .002 from its sibling T1048.001 (Symmetric). verified_correct: All 18 flagged MITRE technique IDs exist, are current, and are non-deprecated in Enterprise v19.1: T1566.002 Spearphishing Link, T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle, T1539 Steal Web Session Cookie, T1550.004 Use Alternate Authentication Material: Web Session Cookie, T1078.004 Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts, T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application, T1505.003 Web Shell, T1552.001 Credentials In Files, T1213.003 Code Repositories, T1098.001 Additional Cloud Credentials, T1530 Data from Cloud Storage, T1204.002 User Execution: Malicious File, T1548.002 Bypass User Account Control, T1555.003 Credentials from Web Browsers, T1528 Steal Application Access Token, T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact, T1133 External Remote Services, T1048.002 (ID valid; only the displayed name is truncated).

T1213.003 (Code Repositories) is correctly scoped for R3 — the MITRE page confirms it covers GitHub/GitLab/BitBucket source-code repos and unsecured credentials; the demo's decision to keep it for R3 is right.
v19.1 is a real, current released MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise version (v19 = April 2026 release), so the version label the demo cites is accurate.

SOC 2 CC6.7 semantics are correct: CC6.7 governs restriction/protection of information during transmission, movement, and removal — not authentication. Dropping it from R1 MFA is the right call (AICPA 2017 TSC text confirms).

SOC 2 CC9.2 semantics are correct: CC9.2 is 'assesses and manages risks associated with vendors and business partners.' Dropping it from R5 employee/contractor offboarding is right; keeping only for vendor-engaged contractors is a defensible nuance.
SOC 2 CC6.2 is correctly identified as the leaver criterion — it covers registering/authorizing new users and removing credentials when access is no longer authorized.
SOC 2 CC6.8 'exact anchor' claim for R4 EDR is correct — CC6.8 covers preventing/detecting/acting on unauthorized or malicious software.
SOC 2 A1.2 (backup + recovery infrastructure) and A1.3 (testing recovery-plan procedures) are correctly characterized; adding A1.3 restore-testing to R3 is a legitimate Type I gap-closure.

The SOC 2 evidence matrix corrections overall are sound: two drops (CC6.7, CC9.2), two adds (CC6.3 to R2, A1.3 to R3), and the honest CC7.x monitoring/IR caveat are all consistent with AICPA TSC text.
data.summary: The SOC 2 / AICPA TSC side of the mapping is solid — every criterion semantic the demo asserts (CC6.7 = transmission/removal, CC9.2 = vendor risk, CC6.2 = leaver, CC6.8 = malicious software, A1.2/A1.3 = backup/recovery-testing) checks out against authoritative sources, and its two drops / two adds are all correct. The MITRE side is where the demo's headline claim collapses: all 18 technique IDs are valid and current, but the demo's proudly-advertised 'second expert pass correction' — replacing the R2/R5 database-collection mapping with prose because 'ATT&CK has no precise SQL-dump Collection technique' — is factually wrong. T1213.006 'Data from Information Repositories: Databases' has existed since May 2025, is current in the exact v19.1 the demo verified against, and explicitly names PostgreSQL and Azure SQL Database. The demo also mis-scopes the T1213 parent as excluding relational databases when the parent page itself includes them. This is a critical credibility defect because it's the one place the demo brags about catching a mistake, and the 'fix' introduced a new, checkable error. Secondary: a research-JSON-vs-ROADMAP contradiction on whether T1213 was 'verified' or 'a misfit' (major), a dropped 'Asymmetric' in the T1048.002 name (minor), and an overbroad uniform last-modified date (cosmetic, T1098.001 is 2025-10-24).

data.area: Security substance (5 risks, mitigations/controls, KRIs, sequencing, threat model) + the internal-consistency of the headline numbers that carry the security story

defects.what: MITRE technique T1048.002 is given an imprecise name in the ROADMAP kill-chain tables — 'Exfiltration Over Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol' — dropping the qualifier that distinguishes the sub-technique. The correct name is 'Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol' (.001 is Symmetric). The deliverable's own research file uses the correct full name, so ROADMAP is internally inconsistent with outputs/09.

defects.proof: Verified against attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1048/002/: full name is 'Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol' (ATT&CK Enterprise v19.1, consistent with the deliverable's stated version).
defects.fix: In ROADMAP.md name T1048.002 'Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol' to match outputs/09 and the live ATT&CK page.

verified_correct: Composite scores are computed with primary-impact weight 0.6 / secondary 0.4 (not the 0.4/0.6 a naive reader might assume): recomputed all five (R1 15.3, R2 15.2, R3 17.6, R4 12.6, R5 11.9) and they match outputs/02 and the framework_sensitivity 0.6 row exactly. Do not 'correct' the impact weights.

advanced_analytics.py is deterministic and reproduces outputs/08 exactly on re-run (both v1.0 and v1.1 blocks); aggregate 65.7%->25.5% (v1.0) and 56.7%->20.5% (v1.1), residual math residual=baseline*(1-effectiveness) all check out per-risk (e.g. R3 20*(1-.70)=6.0, R1 30*(1-.80)=6.0).

The named security controls are current best practice and correctly scoped: phishing-resistant MFA via Entra CA authentication strengths (origin-bound FIDO2/passkeys defeat AiTM by protocol design); GitHub Actions OIDC workload-identity federation to eliminate long-lived service-principal secrets + least-privilege RBAC; Azure WAF with origin lock via X-Azure-FDID/service tag; PostgreSQL least-privilege role + RLS; Defender for Business as the sub-300-seat EDR SKU; Windows LAPS + ASR rules (block LSASS credential theft, Office child processes, email/webmail executable content); Entra ID Governance Lifecycle Workflows on employeeLeaveDateTime; immutable WORM blob backups + Azure Backup vault with multi-user authorization. None is outdated or dangerous.

The known technical sharp edges are all explicitly flagged in the feasibility reviews rather than glossed: RLS per-request SET tenant context breaking under PgBouncer transaction pooling (04 line 286); compliant-device CA + break-glass account lockout risk and the need for two excluded emergency accounts (04 lines 176, 194); Entra-auth-to-Postgres 60-90 min token refresh breaking connection pools with a Key Vault-password fallback (04 lines 82, 490); quick-win #4 Postgres network lockdown colliding with contractor direct-DB accounts, correctly gated behind R5 (04 lines 84, 93); EPM-vs-exception-group for local-admin removal (04 line 388). These are correct, not defects.

The MITRE mapping correction is sound and the version claim checks out: T1213 (Data from Information Repositories) is correctly scoped by MITRE to SharePoint/Confluence/wikis/code repos and was correctly removed from the R2/R5 SQL-dump rows; T1213.003 (Code Repositories) correctly retained for R3; ATT&CK Enterprise v19.1 is the current version (released April 28, 2026) so 'v19.1, pages last modified 2026-05-12' is plausible and consistent.

The SOC 2 criterion corrections are correct against AICPA TSC text: dropping CC6.7 from R1 (it governs transmission/movement/removal of data, not authentication) and dropping CC9.2 from R5 (it is vendor/business-partner risk; employee offboarding is CC6.2/CC6.3) are both right; adding A1.3 (recovery-plan testing) to R3 is a legitimate Type-I gap fix.

The R3->rank-1 challenge/defense pair is internally coherent and the framework_sensitivity sweep (primary weight 0.30-0.70) legitimately shows R3 stays #1 across all tested weights and under mean-vs-median; the R1-vs-R2 near-tie (gap 0.1) is correctly flagged as noise.

SharePoint/OneDrive recovery defaults cited are correct: 500 versions default, 93-day recycle-bin retention, 30-day OneDrive Files Restore / SharePoint 'Restore this library' window; the residual-gap that post-30-day dwell is unrecoverable is correctly stated (04 line 393).

The pricing corrections in outputs/09 are internally consistent and the preserved pre-audit values in outputs/04 (e.g. EPM $3.60 vs corrected $3.00; 'E5 Security' vs 'Defender Suite') are explicitly labeled as preserved as-run agent output, not silently wrong.
data.summary: Acting as the vCISO / Azure-M365 architect, I read the full security substance — all five risks (outputs/01), all mitigations and named controls (outputs/04), the five IT-feasibility reviews, the KRIs and three-wave sequencing (ROADMAP.md), the threat model, the MITRE and SOC 2 mappings, and the quant model — and re-ran the analytics code. The security substance is strong and defensible: every recommended control is current best practice, correctly licensed, and right-sized for a 3-person IT team; no control is technically wrong, outdated, or dangerous; the threat chains are plausible; and every genuinely hard technical edge (RLS under PgBouncer, compliant-device lockout/break-glass, Entra-auth-to-Postgres token refresh, the contractor-Postgres vs network-lockdown collision, EPM vs exception groups) is explicitly flagged rather than glossed. I found no dangerous recommendation and no implausible threat chain. The real, provable defects are numeric/labeling inconsistencies in the headline artifacts, and notably the deliverable's OWN critique file (outputs/10) already catches the two biggest ones (exec summary R1 -11.7pp vs computed 11.8pp; exec summary body shipping superseded v1.0 numbers with no inline pointer to v1.1) and ships them UNFIXED — which is the sharpest credibility hit because it directly undercuts the 'every number traces to code/JSON' pitch. Plus a cosmetic MITRE T1048.002 name imprecision in ROADMAP that disagrees with the deliverable's own research file. None of these change the ranking or the fund-R1-and-R3 conclusion. I verified and confirmed a large set of load-bearing items (composite weighting, residual math, control correctness, feasibility caveats, MITRE/SOC 2 corrections) as correct so a fix pass does not disturb them.

synthesis.verdict: The demo is analytically strong and its external citations are impeccable, but it ships two credibility-damaging errors — a residual-EAL math bug that understates every ROSI figure, and a flagship MITRE "correction" that is itself factually wrong — plus a cluster of SOC 2 matrix transcription mismatches that contradict the deliverable's own "dropped 2 / added 2" boast.

confirmed_defects.what: The demo's headline MITRE 'we caught a mistake' correction is itself wrong. ROADMAP.md:55 claims 'ATT&CK has no precise "dump a SQL database" Collection technique' and that MITRE scopes T1213 to SharePoint/Confluence/wikis/code repos 'not a backend relational database', then replaces the R2/R5 database-collection mapping with prose. Verified live: T1213.006 'Data from Information Repositories: Databases' (created 2025-05-22, current in v19.1, not deprecated, Collection tactic TA0009) explicitly names PostgreSQL and Azure SQL Database — the exact fit. The one place the demo brags about rigor introduced a new, checkable error.
confirmed_defects.where: ROADMAP.md:55 (false 'no precise Collection technique' + T1213 parent-scope claim); prose placeholders at ROADMAP.md:60 and :63
confirmed_defects.fix: Map R2 and R5 database-collection steps to T1213.006 (https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1213/006/) Data from Information Repositories: Databases; delete the 'no precise ATT&CK Collection ID for a SQL dump' sentence at :55 and the two prose placeholders at :60/:63; remove the false claim that T1213 excludes relational/backend databases.

confirmed_defects.what: Residual EAL is computed as a cross-paired vertex average — resid_mean = (bl*(1-eh)+bm*(1-em)+bh*(1-el))/3 — which pairs the low baseline with high effectiveness (perfect negative correlation). Baseline and effectiveness are independent everywhere else in the model, so E[residual] = mean_b*(1-mean_e). A 1.5M-draw Monte Carlo confirms the true value (R3 = 7.555) matches product-of-means (7.556), not the shipped cross-paired 9.733. This overstates residual EAL ~20-37% per risk, understating avoided EAL and thus every ROSI figure. The function's own eal_basis string (line 213) claims 'probability = triangular mean', which the code contradicts. Confirmed: working tree is clean (no uncommitted fix exists — the shipped/HEAD code is the buggy version).

The deterministic quant model (v1.0 and v1.1), aggregate 1-Prod(1-p), baseline/residual bounds, marginal/standalone contributions, tornado, framework sensitivity sweep (R3 stays #1 across 0.30-0.70), the ~0.40 correlated-MC correlation, loss-exceedance, and risk-appetite verdicts all reproduce exactly. The residual-bounds cross-pairing (low_b x high_e best case, high_b x low_e worst case) is the CORRECT bounding convention and is distinct from the EAL-mean bug — do not touch it.

The baseline EAL leg (prob_base_mean = (bl+bm+bh)/3) and the ~13.6% mode-vs-mean baseline correction are correct. Only the residual leg (line 198) is wrong. Do not disturb the baseline computation while fixing line 198.

ALL external factual citations verified against primary 2024-2026 sources and are accurate: Proofpoint 62%/65% ATO, Google/NYU/UCSD 2019 security-key 100%, Verizon DBIR 2026 (48%/31%/96%), GitGuardian 2026 (28.65M/64%), Sophos $638,536, NetDiligence 2025 ($142K Nano-Rev, $2.1M BI-ransomware, confirmed from primary PDF), IBM CoDB 2025 ($160/record; 'CoDB 2026 not yet published' caveat correct), Microsoft Tycoon2FA March 2026 blog, cyber-insurance underwriting, and all vendor pricing values ($10 Entra P2, $12 Defender Suite, $19 GitHub Secret Protection on Team, $330 Front Door Premium, $58/$29 YubiKey, $0 Postgres Entra). SOC 2 criterion SEMANTICS are all correct (CC6.7=transmission/removal, CC9.2=vendor risk, CC6.2=leaver, CC6.8=malicious software, A1.2/A1.3=backup/recovery-testing); the two drops (CC6.7, CC9.2) and two adds (CC6.3, A1.3) are the right calls. The pre-audit over-claims (R1->CC6.7, R5->CC9.2) preserved unedited in outputs/04, and the corrected v1.1 pricing/parameter values, are intentionally kept as as-run artifacts — do not overwrite them; the correction lives in the ROADMAP matrix / SOURCES by design.

Do not manufacture the outputs/10 self-critique items as new defects — outputs/10 is a shipped artifact and its mode-vs-mean, RNG-threading, and labeling notes are disclosures, not open bugs (and it did NOT catch the actual residual cross-pairing bug, so that fix is the genuinely new one).
synthesis.headline: Essentially sound demo with rigorous citations and reproducible math, marred by exactly two credibility-grade errors — a residual-EAL cross-pairing bug and a flagship MITRE 'correction' that is itself wrong (T1213.006 exists) — plus SOC 2 matrix transcription mismatches; six targeted fixes make it airtight without touching the strong analytical core.
